lessons.title: Positional Notation, Radix & Base Conversion
lessons.content:
Every number system you use is **positional**: a digit's weight is the radix raised to its position index. In radix $r$, the value of a string is $\sum_{i} d_i \cdot r^{i}$, where $i$ counts positions from $0$ at the right. Decimal is just $r=10$; binary is $r=2$; octal $r=8$; hex $r=16$. So $\mathtt{1011}_2 = 1\cdot2^3 + 0\cdot2^2 + 1\cdot2^1 + 1\cdot2^0 = 11_{10}$.

**Hex is the human-readable face of binary** because $16 = 2^4$: every hex digit maps to exactly one nibble (4 bits), so you regroup bits four at a time with zero arithmetic. $\mathtt{0xB7} = \mathtt{1011\,0111}_2$. Octal ($2^3$) does the same in 3-bit groups and survives mainly in Unix file permissions. To go **binary to hex**, pad to a multiple of 4 bits and translate each group; to go **hex to binary**, expand each digit to 4 bits.

Converting **decimal to binary** uses repeated division: divide by 2, record remainders, read them bottom-up. Going the other way, just sum the set bit-weights. A bit is one binary digit; a **nibble** is 4 bits, a **byte** is 8, and a **word** is whatever your architecture calls native (16/32/64). **Endianness** describes byte ordering in memory: big-endian stores the most-significant byte first, little-endian the least-significant first. The value $\mathtt{0x12345678}$ on a little-endian machine sits in memory as bytes $\mathtt{78\,56\,34\,12}$ — a classic source of bugs when you cast a pointer or read a network packet.

items.front: In radix $r$, what is the value of a digit string $d_n \dots d_1 d_0$?
items.back: $\sum_{i=0}^{n} d_i \cdot r^{i}$ — each digit is weighted by the radix raised to its position index (counting from 0 at the right).
items.hint: Position index is the exponent on the base.

items.front: Why is hexadecimal the natural shorthand for binary?
items.back: Because $16 = 2^4$: each hex digit corresponds to exactly one 4-bit nibble, so converting is pure regrouping with no arithmetic.
items.hint: Think about $16$ as a power of 2.

items.front: Convert $\mathtt{1011\,0111}_2$ to hexadecimal.
items.back: $\mathtt{1011} = \mathtt{B}$, $\mathtt{0111} = 7$, so the value is $\mathtt{0xB7}$.
items.hint: Split into nibbles and translate each.

items.front: What is the repeated-division method for decimal-to-binary conversion?
items.back: Repeatedly divide by 2, recording each remainder (0 or 1). Read the remainders from last to first (bottom-up) to get the binary digits.

items.front: Define big-endian vs little-endian byte ordering.
items.back: Big-endian stores the most-significant byte at the lowest address; little-endian stores the least-significant byte first. E.g. $\mathtt{0x12345678}$ little-endian = bytes $\mathtt{78\,56\,34\,12}$.
items.hint: Which byte lands at the lowest address?

items.prompt: What is the decimal value of $\mathtt{1011}_2$?
How many binary bits does a single hexadecimal digit represent?
items.explanation: $16 = 2^4$, so each hex digit maps to exactly 4 bits (one nibble).

items.prompt: When converting binary to octal, bits are grouped in sets of how many?
items.explanation: $8 = 2^3$, so octal groups bits three at a time, in contrast to hex's four.

items.prompt: A little-endian machine stores the 32-bit value $\mathtt{0x0A0B0C0D}$. Which byte is at the lowest memory address?
items.explanation: Little-endian places the least-significant byte ($\mathtt{0x0D}$) at the lowest address.

lessons.title: Binary Arithmetic, Signed Representations & Overflow
lessons.content:
Binary addition works column-by-column with carries, exactly like decimal: $1+1 = \mathtt{10}$ (write 0, carry 1). The interesting question is how to represent **negatives**. Three schemes compete. **Sign-magnitude** dedicates the MSB to sign and leaves the rest as magnitude — simple to read but it has two zeros ($+0$ and $-0$) and needs separate add/subtract logic. **One's complement** negates by flipping every bit; it also has a $\pm0$ problem and an awkward end-around carry.

**Two's complement won** because it has a single zero and ordinary binary addition "just works" across the sign boundary — the same adder hardware handles signed and unsigned. To negate, invert all bits and add 1: $-x = \overline{x} + 1$. For an $n$-bit word the representable range is $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$ — asymmetric, with one extra negative value. The MSB carries weight $-2^{n-1}$, so $\mathtt{1000}_2 = -8$ in 4 bits.

**Overflow detection** is where ex-hardware folks must stay sharp: **carry-out and signed overflow are different flags**. Carry-out (C) is the carry beyond the MSB and signals *unsigned* overflow. Signed **overflow (V)** occurs when two same-sign operands produce a wrong-sign result; equivalently, $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ at the MSB. Adding $+5 + +4$ in 4 bits gives $\mathtt{1001} = -7$: V is set, C is clear. **Sign extension** preserves value when widening: replicate the MSB into the new high bits ($\mathtt{1011} \to \mathtt{1111\,1011}$).

items.front: How do you negate a two's-complement number?
items.back: Invert every bit and add 1: $-x = \overline{x} + 1$. The same operation applied twice returns the original value.

items.front: Why did two's complement win over sign-magnitude and one's complement?
items.back: It has a single representation of zero, and ordinary binary addition works correctly across the sign boundary — one adder handles both signed and unsigned arithmetic.
items.hint: Think about zero and reusing the adder.

items.front: What is the representable range of an $n$-bit two's-complement integer?
items.back: $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$. It is asymmetric: one more negative value than positive because of the single zero.

items.front: Give a formula for the signed overflow (V) flag in an adder.
items.back: $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ at the most-significant bit. Equivalently, V is set when two same-sign operands yield a result of the opposite sign.
items.hint: XOR of the two carries into and out of the MSB.

items.front: How do you sign-extend a two's-complement number to a wider word?
items.back: Replicate the sign bit (MSB) into all the new high-order bits. E.g. $\mathtt{1011}$ (=-5) extends to $\mathtt{1111\,1011}$.

items.prompt: What decimal value does $\mathtt{1000}_2$ represent as a 4-bit two's-complement number?
items.explanation: The MSB weight is $-2^3 = -8$ and all other bits are 0, so the value is $-8$ — the most negative 4-bit value.

items.prompt: Adding 4-bit two's-complement $0101 (+5)$ and $0100 (+4)$ yields $1001$. Which flags are correct?
items.explanation: Two positives produced a negative result ($-7$): signed overflow V is set, but no carry leaves the MSB so C is clear.

items.prompt: Which statement about carry-out (C) and signed overflow (V) is correct?
choices: C signals unsigned overflow; V signals signed overflow
V signals unsigned overflow; C signals signed overflow
Only V exists in two's complement
items.explanation: Carry-out indicates the result exceeded the unsigned range; V indicates the signed result has the wrong sign. They are independent flags.

items.prompt: In 8-bit two's complement, what is the representation of $-1$?
items.explanation: Negate $+1$ ($\mathtt{0000\,0001}$): invert to $\mathtt{1111\,1110}$, add 1 to get $\mathtt{1111\,1111}$.

lessons.title: Fixed-Point (Q-Format), BCD & Gray Code
lessons.content:
When you need fractions but cannot afford floating-point hardware — the daily reality of **DSP and radar signal processing [→08]** — you use **fixed-point**. The bits are still a plain integer; you merely *agree* on an implied binary point. **Q-format** names the split: **Q$m$.$n$** has $m$ integer bits and $n$ fraction bits, with the fraction contributing weights $2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \dots, 2^{-n}$. The ubiquitous **Q1.15** packs a sign bit and 15 fraction bits into 16 bits, representing values in $[-1,\ 1)$ with resolution $2^{-15}$.

The key discipline is tracking the format through operations. **Addition** requires identical Q-formats and keeps the format. **Multiplication adds the formats**: a Q1.15 $\times$ Q1.15 product is Q2.30 in a 32-bit register, which you typically shift left by 1 and take the high word to renormalize back to Q1.15. Forgetting that re-scale is the classic fixed-point bug. **Saturating arithmetic** clamps out-of-range results to the max/min instead of **wrapping** (modulo rollover) — essential in signal chains where a wrap turns a loud sample into a catastrophic sign flip.

Two special codes round out the picture. **Binary-coded decimal (BCD)** stores each decimal digit in its own nibble ($\mathtt{0010\,0111} = 27$), trading density for trivial decimal display — it survives in 7-segment displays and real-time clocks. **Gray code** changes exactly one bit between adjacent values, eliminating transition glitches; it is the encoding of choice for rotary/position encoders and for multi-bit clock-domain crossing **[→06]**. Convert binary $b$ to Gray with $g = b \oplus (b \gg 1)$.

items.front: What does the notation Q$m$.$n$ mean in fixed-point arithmetic?
items.back: $m$ integer bits and $n$ fraction bits, with an implied binary point between them. The fraction bits carry weights $2^{-1}$ through $2^{-n}$.

items.front: What range and resolution does Q1.15 provide?
items.back: 16 bits = 1 sign bit + 15 fraction bits, representing $[-1,\ 1)$ with resolution (LSB) of $2^{-15}$.

items.front: What is the output format of a Q1.15 $\times$ Q1.15 multiplication, and what must you do?
items.back: The raw product is Q2.30 in 32 bits. To return to Q1.15 you shift left by 1 (to drop the redundant sign bit) and take the high 16 bits — i.e. re-scale.
items.hint: Formats add on multiply; then renormalize.

items.front: Contrast saturating and wrapping arithmetic.
items.back: Saturating clamps an out-of-range result to the maximum or minimum value; wrapping lets it roll over modulo the word size. DSP prefers saturation to avoid catastrophic sign flips.

items.front: What is binary-coded decimal (BCD) and where does it survive?
items.back: Each decimal digit is stored in its own 4-bit nibble (e.g. $27 = \mathtt{0010\,0111}$). It survives in 7-segment displays and real-time clocks, where direct decimal readout matters more than density.

items.front: How do you convert a binary number $b$ to Gray code, and why use Gray code?
items.back: $g = b \oplus (b \gg 1)$. Gray code changes only one bit between adjacent values, eliminating multi-bit transition glitches in encoders and clock-domain crossings.

items.prompt: Multiplying two Q1.15 numbers produces a raw product in which format?
items.explanation: Integer and fraction bit counts each add: $1+1=2$ integer bits, $15+15=30$ fraction bits, giving Q2.30 in a 32-bit register.

items.prompt: What decimal number does the BCD byte $\mathtt{1001\,0011}$ represent?
items.explanation: Each nibble is one decimal digit: $\mathtt{1001}=9$ and $\mathtt{0011}=3$, so the value is 93 (not the binary value 147).

items.prompt: What is the defining property of Gray code that makes it ideal for encoders and CDC?
choices: It is the densest possible code
Adjacent values differ in exactly one bit
It has no representation of zero
It uses fewer bits than binary
items.explanation: Only one bit changes between consecutive Gray values, so a sampled transition can never catch an intermediate multi-bit glitch.

items.prompt: In an 8-bit unsigned saturating adder, what is $200 + 100$?
items.explanation: 300 exceeds the 8-bit max of 255, so saturating arithmetic clamps the result to 255 rather than wrapping to 44.

lessons.title: IEEE 754 Floating-Point
lessons.content:
Floating-point trades uniform resolution for enormous dynamic range by storing numbers in scientific notation. **IEEE 754 single precision** packs 32 bits as a $1$-bit **sign** $S$, an $8$-bit biased **exponent** $E$, and a $23$-bit **mantissa** (fraction) $M$. For normalized numbers the value is

$$x = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - 127)}$$

The leading $1$ is **implicit** (the "hidden bit"), buying a free 24th bit of precision. The exponent uses a **bias** of $127$ (single) or $1023$ (double) so the stored field stays unsigned while representing both positive and negative exponents. Double precision is the same scheme with $11$ exponent bits and a $52$-bit mantissa.

The exponent field's extremes are reserved. $E = 0$ encodes **zero** (with $M=0$) and **denormals/subnormals** (with $M \ne 0$), which drop the implicit 1 to represent values gradually underflowing toward zero. $E = 255$ (all ones) encodes $\pm\infty$ (with $M=0$) and **NaN** (Not-a-Number, $M \ne 0$), the result of $0/0$ or $\sqrt{-1}$. NaN famously compares unequal to everything, including itself.

Because the mantissa is binary, decimal fractions like $0.1$ have **no exact representation** — $0.1$ is a repeating binary fraction, so it is rounded to the nearest representable value, which is why $0.1 + 0.2 \ne 0.3$ in float. The default rounding mode is **round-to-nearest, ties-to-even**. As a returning engineer, the practical takeaways are: never test floats for exact equality, watch for catastrophic cancellation when subtracting near-equal values, and reach for fixed-point when you need deterministic, exact-scaled arithmetic on an FPGA.

items.front: What are the three fields of an IEEE 754 single-precision number and their widths?
items.back: Sign $S$ (1 bit), biased exponent $E$ (8 bits), and mantissa/fraction $M$ (23 bits), for 32 bits total.

items.front: Write the value formula for a normalized IEEE 754 single-precision number.
items.back: $x = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - 127)}$. The leading 1 is implicit and the exponent bias is 127.

items.front: Why does IEEE 754 store the exponent with a bias instead of two's complement?
items.back: Bias keeps the stored exponent field unsigned (so float magnitude comparison can use integer comparison), while still representing both positive and negative exponents. Single uses bias 127, double uses 1023.
items.hint: Keeps the field unsigned and orderable.

items.front: How are $\pm\infty$ and NaN encoded in IEEE 754?
items.back: Exponent field all ones ($E=255$ in single): mantissa $M=0$ gives $\pm\infty$, while $M \ne 0$ gives NaN. NaN compares unequal to everything, including itself.
items.hint: Max exponent; mantissa zero vs nonzero.

items.front: What do denormal (subnormal) numbers represent and how are they encoded?
items.back: $E = 0$ with $M \ne 0$. They drop the implicit leading 1, allowing gradual underflow — representing very small magnitudes below the smallest normalized value down toward zero.
items.hint: Exponent field zero, nonzero mantissa, no hidden bit.

items.front: Why is $0.1$ not exactly representable in binary floating-point?
items.back: $0.1$ is a repeating fraction in binary (like $1/3$ in decimal), so it is rounded to the nearest representable value. This is why $0.1 + 0.2 \ne 0.3$ exactly.

items.prompt: How many exponent bits and what bias does IEEE 754 double precision use?
items.explanation: Double precision has 11 exponent bits with a bias of 1023, plus a 52-bit mantissa and 1 sign bit (64 bits total).

items.prompt: The 'hidden bit' in normalized IEEE 754 numbers refers to what?
choices: An implicit leading 1 before the mantissa's binary point
items.explanation: Normalized values are $1.M$; the leading 1 is implied and not stored, giving 24 bits of effective precision in single from a 23-bit field.

items.prompt: Which expression is true for an IEEE 754 NaN value x?
items.explanation: NaN is unordered: every comparison except inequality returns false, so $x == x$ is false. This is the standard way to detect NaN.

items.prompt: What is the default IEEE 754 rounding mode?
items.explanation: Round-to-nearest, ties-to-even (banker's rounding) is the default; it minimizes statistical bias when ties occur.
